speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about your work with the United States Holocaust Council and the building of the museum. And at the point we left, we had an empty lot on-- on the mall and plans to search for an architect, I believe is where we were.

speaker: interviewee
text: And an architect was found and several other architects were found, until we found Jim Freed, who was with Pei, IM Pei. And after much, much pain-- and we're the same age, he was also a refugee who had suppressed a great deal of his experience-- he came up with a concept that bowled us over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the-- the committee that dealt with the exhibits worked many, many hours to come up with-- and drafted revisions, many of them with the intention of trying to replicate the experience not only of the Holocaust, but of the-- the culture and civilization that existed before the Holocaust. In other words, what were the pre-Nazi periods like in Poland, Germany, Russia where the victims ultimately came from? And that had to be designed very artistically.

speaker: interviewee
text: At some point, those of us who were involved in the Education Committee-- I was the first chairman of the Education Committee and then the Academic Committee-- wanted to get a-- an original car in which Jews were transported from one of the trains. So we asked the Polish government would they contribute a car. They said they'd be glad to do that. However, [LAUGHS] when the car arrived, it was painted and lacquered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had destroyed the symbol completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: They'd restored it, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You know, they had-- had missed the whole point. [SIGHS] So here we had to de-restore it, this cattle car, which had been used to transport humans to their-- to their ultimate destination of-- of execution.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the decision, what parts of the museum [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] would be possible for children to see? You know, what parts you'd want to restrict because-- what were the age limits you'd want to encourage or discourage? So these were all issues that were very carefully discussed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then ultimately we created a research institute for the museum to deal with an ongoing body of research to encourage scholars to deal with new evidence. We commissioned replication of documents and got millions of linear feet of documentation after negotiations with the Polish government, Soviet-- former Soviet Union, Czechoslovakia, Romania, all those countries in which Hitler's Final Solution was-- was perpetrated. And now we have ongoing projects to get this material [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] organized in such a way as to be usable for scholarly purposes for the next generation or generations. We also have a vigorous program for high school students and for seminarians and for laypeople. And the lions in front of the museum, day in and day out, they give evidence to the fact that there is interest and that the museum has developed the reputation as the place that must be visited when you go to Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: You must be very proud of-- of the-- the effort you put in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I am, and it's an ongoing one, since I was just reappointed by President Clinton to the United States Holocaust Memorial Council, which, from the time of President Carter to the time of President Reagan's reappointment, extended for 12 years, and now will resume for another five. Well, now will be the time to really test the mettle of the museum, because there will come a hiatus at some point, and we have to rethink the whole educational program of Holocaust studies and Holocaust education.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the survivors will be passing from our midst, too. And how do we pass the torch for the guidance of the museum to those who are not from the survivor generation is a problem, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think a lot of-- a lot of the education will have to take place audiovisually, the way we're doing this today. It is some of the cameo pieces that are already features of the Museum of Survivors, those who-- who survived the horrors of the camps, so poignant and powerful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We recently had an exhibit on American soldiers who opened the camps, what they saw. And that was perhaps more compelling to Americans, because these were faces they recognized as their neighbors. And I remember when-- when we had one of our very first meetings at the White House. It was right after the attempted assassination of President Reagan, and we weren't sure whether the president was able to come or not. He had not been seen in public. No one knew the true extent of his injuries. And suddenly the doors opened and in walked the president and Elie Wiesel, at which point one of his aides put a speech on the podium. And when he got there, he pushed it aside, and he pulled out his famous 5-by-8 cards, and he began to talk. And at that point, the issue was revisionism. Several books had just come out denying that the Holocaust existed. And the president then said that he had been in charge of, every day, putting together, editing the rushes that came in from the front, and that night they would be presented to the general staff. And that he had seen the rushes of the opening of the concentration camp Bergen-Belsen by the British, other camps by Americans, and he had seen the horrors of the camps himself. And therefore, he was answering directly. And he got off his sick bed to do that. That was one of the most memorable hours, I think, that I've ever spent in my life. Because I was standing right next to him. I was invited to deliver the opening remarks that day. So I had just finished them when the doors opened up and in walked Elie and the president. And he got up there and he spoke, and he was pale and perspiring and weak. But this was a message he had to-- he had to deliver. And it was a powerful, poignant time in the development of our relations with the White House.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Interestingly enough, it was President Bush and President Reagan who were the staunchest supporters of the museum as it was being built.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at this point, President Clinton and Vice President Gore have been to the museum at least four or five times personally, either to have taken heads of state there or at-- at the beginning, Gore and Clinton went by themselves to see what was in the museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Extraordinary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Gottschalk, you've had such a rich career as a Zionist, as an educator, as the builder of a national, living monument and-- and memorial. What are the things in your life you are most proud of? Can you talk and select among the many things you've done and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm--

speaker: interviewer
text: --describe them to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: --very proud of my-- my family, most proud, the children and grandchildren, who are pursuing serious interests and are good people. That's number one. Then professionally, I think the facilitation of the first-- of women to study within our school was a very important thing for me and the ordination of the first woman as a rabbi in the Jewish experience,

speaker: interviewer
text: You are the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in a formal sense.

speaker: interviewer
text: --rabbi who did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- it is the 25th anniversary this year of the ordination of Sally Priesand. And then the ordination of Naamah Kelman as the first woman rabbi in Israel, the beginnings of the Jewish Communal School where none existed, serious programs in higher Jewish education in Los Angeles to the PhD level where none existed, and just the ability to do an experiment with educational philosophy represents those things that I'm most proud of. And of course, the Jerusalem school and its entire program, its archaeological outreach, its cultural outreach, its center in the heartland of the-- of the Jewish world, and its ability to speak to Reform Jews and others, Jews and non-Jews about the importance of the experience of Jews living in antiquity and in different cultures, this is terribly significant for us. And I think as an American, a quintessential American, I feel very closely to the American experience, that the Jew can add a dimension to American democracy because of the suffering that the Jew has experienced--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the tolerance towards the immigrant. I am appalled by some of the legislation that's being contemplated in California not to give educational opportunities to the children of so-called illegal immigrants, or illegal immigrants. This is absolutely against the fabric of the American spirit, and this makes one furious.

speaker: interviewer
text: So having gone through what one went through and experienced that component, it gives you the fire in the belly to go on and fight for democratic principles and human rights. I believe that's our major purpose. Having been spared extinction, we need to help others survive. So your-- your work has been an expression-- all aspects of your work has been an expression of your experience as a survivor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's my grandfather's words, you see, piecing together those scraps of parchment of Torah that were cut asunder, you know, realigning the-- the fragments to make them into a whole. I think that's been my motivating spirit and vision.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Powerful image. Dr. Gottschalk, this film will be seen, we hope, by many people far in the future. What message, beyond what you've said or in addition to what you've said, do you wish to leave with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me just go back to something that the very great rabbi who has survived Theresienstadt gave us as a legacy, and he called it the 11th commandment. He says, 'Despite all, thou shalt hope.' See? I think that's-- no matter how bad it-- it looks and-- and it really is, you cannot give up tikvah-- hope. [HEBREW], our hope was never lost. And it can't be lost for the human family in the future either. We just have to gather what we have learned from the past and use it to address the present with passion and with vision.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do we [INAUDIBLE]--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ann's easy to talk to. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Gottschalk, would you introduce to us this lovely lady?

speaker: interviewee
text: This lovely lady is my wife Deanna. And she accompanied me with great enthusiasm when we made our first trip to Eastern Europe on behalf of the United States Holocaust Memorial Commission at the time and went to experience the horrors of Auschwitz and Treblinka. It was a-- really a soul-searing experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can-- can you tell us a little bit about what your reaction was and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --specifically as the wife of a survivor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. That has been very painful for me, because I was born in 1939, which is something that has always made me feel uncomfortable that, you know, that would be at that time and Fred having those following years so difficult. Actually, I guess, before everything-- for a while things were quite well, I guess. But it's just been very sad to realize what happened during that period, that when I was born and thinking of my family, my own family, having a very different kind of life. And-- and it did-- it does make me sad. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] You know, I-- I would have wished that I would have been able to be, you know, with him or closer at least, and I just-- I had no choice. I had no idea. And I think probably one of the first things that came to my mind growing up was of the death of Franklin Roosevelt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I guess around that time, it was maybe when I finally began to catch on a little bit of what was-- what was happening. But already so much had almost, you know, finished by 1945. And so yes, that was a, I think, my life since I've known Fred, that's been something that's been sad for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: However, I was very fortunate to-- to know Fred's mother, whom I loved very much, and that part, I think, was quite wonderful for me to get to know her. And I think she and I were very close, and we loved each other. But still, you know, it's not the same kind of situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both of you as parents, how-- how do you talk about and pass on this experience to the next generation of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very grateful for the fact, particularly my oldest son Andrew, who is very-- well, both of my sons, I think, are very concerned and interested. But Charlie-- my older son Andrew, actually, he's been very involved in-- in all of this, and particularly with the-- you know, he has a coffeehouse in-- in Washington. [LAUGHS] So we're there when we can be. And he has learned quite a bit from being connected with the museum, and that's been a great pleasure for me and for him, you know, for us to at least have that aspect of-- of life as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you both very much for speaking with us this afternoon. It's been an honor to spend this time with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Gottschalk, tell us about this picture, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Oberwesel am Rhein looking up the river towards Sankt Goar, which is over here, and the Lorelei, which is around the bend. And our home was somewhere over here near the-- near the river, and the synagogue was over here in this plaza-- picture-- this part of the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father floated up the river to-- down the river--

speaker: interviewee
text: Down the river.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to-- to Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are the boats that were coming-- like barges, as you see from this window here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Kirchstrasse, and this is Kirchstrasse number 19, which was the house in which we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you occupy the entire house?

speaker: interviewee
text: The entire house. Behind it was a garden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of my father's parents, Jakob Gottschalk of Niederzissen. He was born January 1-- January 21, 1868. And according to the records of the museum, the Holocaust Museum, he died in Minsk in a deportation. His wife was Minna Gottschalk, and she was born November 24, 1870. She also died in Minsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- the person to the immediate left is my father. In the center of the picture is his sister Frieda Lillianthal and his younger brother Sauly Gottschalk. Frieda, we now know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was killed in Minsk. Her birth date was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] October 30, '99. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And Sauly Gottschalk, we have an International Red Cross report on that he was deported from the concentration camp at [INAUDIBLE] and was taken to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father's passport picture done in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph taken in Amsterdam by a-- a relative. It's a picture of my father with a cousin who lived in Amsterdam. This is a photograph of my mother's family. Seated at the table with the white lace are her parents, August Trum and Rosalie Trum. Standing between August and Rosalie is my mother. To her left is sister Elsa and then her brother Ernst. Elsa died in Theresienstadt, Ernst of natural causes in Buenos Aires. To the immediate left of my grandfather is the youngest sister Alice, who is in New York, and Rosel, who, together with her husband, to the best of our knowledge, died in one of the concentration camps. This is a family photograph, same family minus my grandfather August taken maybe 20 years earlier, of my grandmother on the left, Elsa, the oldest; Erna, my mother, in the middle; Alice, the youngest; and Ernst standing there with a cap. Henrietta Gerson. She was the mother of the twin boys, Alfred and Berthold, who were killed in World War I, and the wife of Gustav Gerson. She had been, in the late '20s, mayor of Oberwesel. This is Alfred Gerson, one of the twins who was killed in World War I on the Eastern Front. His brother Berthold was also killed within the same week in Kaiser Wilhelm's army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a second. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of my mother's cooking class. This was a finishing school in Frankfurt, and she was a great baker and cook. She learned it the right way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one is your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one, this lady right here, modestly hiding in the back behind the third pot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just one second.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beautiful embroidery on that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my Aunt Elsa, who was the middle sister, Elsa Trum. She, to the best of our knowledge, was deported to Theresienstadt in 1942. This is Rosel, the oldest sister. She and her husband were deported and, to the best of our knowledge, died in Auschwitz. After services on Shabbat, my father would take me for a little walk down near the Rhine. And here we are heading for the Coca-Cola stand, where he made an arrangement with the salesperson there to give me my weekly treat of Coca-Cola. And of course, since he was a traditional Jew, he didn't pay for it at that time. I always felt that he was such a powerful man to get Coca-Colas free.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]. Oh, that's sweet.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken of me when I was about between two and a half and three years old with my favorite teddy bear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: If he did, I don't remember it, but he always needed a bath. This was a picture done in my favorite sailor suit. I would guess I was about six years old, hat to match.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was typical outfit for a little German boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Typical outfit, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken just before we emigrated from Germany in the summer of '39 with my Aunt Elsa, on the immediate left my grandfather Gustav, myself, and my mother standing in front of our house at 19 Kirchstrasse. This is my official bar mitzvah picture, obviously around the age of 13. The ties are back in style.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Where were you bar mitvahed, at what--

speaker: interviewee
text: The 9th Street Temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the 9th Street.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken on the same day as I graduated high school. It was taken at the We were part of an oratorical contest that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead and go through that one more time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken on the day of my high school graduation. But it was taken at the because they had an oratorical contest that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: --public speaker, prized public speaker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, an orator. And I won that debate, so that accounts for the smile on my face.

speaker: interviewer
text: Los Altos.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Photograph was taken in Caesarea on the coast of the Mediterranean in Israel in 1951, my first trip to Israel. This is a picture of Dean Kletzel awarding me my PhD degree at the University of Southern California in the summer of 1965.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the passport my mother was granted, and you see they inserted the name Sara. All Jewish women were given the name Sara. And all Jewish men, boys, were given the name Israel so that anyone reading the passport would know immediately that they're Jewish. This is my mother's passport, and I'm written on here as the attendant child. However, the exit visas were dated at a different time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother immigrated to Buenos Aires in 1937. However, when she wanted to come to America she had to be declared a national. Since her citizenship was revoked in Germany, she had to be granted renewed citizenship status, and here they list her as a German national. This was necessary for her in order to come on immigration visa to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's the only time she traveled on this passport--

speaker: interviewee
text: The only time--

speaker: interviewer
text: --was just that one time.

speaker: interviewee
text: --she traveled on this passport, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Second. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This family portrait was made when my granddaughter Atara was bas mitzvah. On the left is Dee's mother Selma Zefti, myself, my son Marc. Just beneath him here [INAUDIBLE] is Atara Gottschalk, Sara, the youngest, Dania, my daughter Rachel, her husband JB, Marc's wife Debbie, my cousin Evelyn Morcher, her husband Jerry Gurland, and former brother-in-law Mort Schrag.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
